description.task: Use UNION to combine results from two queries and understand the difference between UNION (deduplicates) and UNION ALL (keeps duplicates).
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE current_employees (
  emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name   VARCHAR(60),
  dept   VARCHAR(40),
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'current'
);

CREATE TABLE former_employees (
  emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name   VARCHAR(60),
  dept   VARCHAR(40),
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'former'
);

INSERT INTO current_employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering','current'),
  (2,'Bob',  'Marketing',  'current'),
  (3,'Carol','HR',         'current'),
  (4,'Dave', 'Engineering','current');

INSERT INTO former_employees VALUES
  (101,'Alice','Engineering','former'),  -- Alice also in former (she rejoined)
  (102,'Eve',  'Marketing',  'former'),
  (103,'Frank','HR',         'former');

-- UNION: combine all unique names across both tables
SELECT name, dept, status FROM current_employees
UNION
SELECT name, dept, status FROM former_employees
ORDER BY name;

-- UNION ALL: include ALL rows including duplicates
-- Note: same person can appear twice with different status
SELECT name, dept, status FROM current_employees
UNION ALL
SELECT name, dept, status FROM former_employees
ORDER BY name;

-- Count difference
SELECT 'UNION'     AS type, COUNT(*) AS rows FROM (
  SELECT name,dept,status FROM current_employees UNION     SELECT name,dept,status FROM former_employees
) u
UNION ALL
SELECT 'UNION ALL' AS type, COUNT(*) AS rows FROM (
  SELECT name,dept,status FROM current_employees UNION ALL SELECT name,dept,status FROM former_employees
) ua;
instructions: Run all INSERT and all three queries.
UNION: 6 unique rows (Alice deduplicated — appears once from each table but status differs, so actually kept both).
Add a comment: UNION deduplicates based on ALL selected columns — why does Alice appear in both results?
description.hint: UNION deduplicates by comparing all selected columns. Alice's current and former rows have different 'status' values, so they are NOT identical rows — both are kept.
description.expectedOutput:
UNION: 6 rows (Alice kept twice — different status)
UNION ALL: 7 rows (all rows from both tables)
Count: UNION=6, UNION ALL=7

description.task: Use INTERSECT (common rows between two queries) and EXCEPT (rows in first but not in second) for set-based comparisons.
instructions: Run all INSERT and all three set operation queries.
INTERSECT: Alice, Carol, Eve (in both years) — 3 returning students.
EXCEPT (2025-2026): Bob, Dave (dropped out after 2025) — 2 students.
Add a comment: how would you achieve INTERSECT using a JOIN? And EXCEPT using NOT EXISTS?
description.hint: INTERSECT equivalent: SELECT a.name FROM enrolled_2025 a JOIN enrolled_2026 b ON a.name = b.name. EXCEPT equivalent: SELECT name FROM enrolled_2025 WHERE name NOT IN (SELECT name FROM enrolled_2026).
description.expectedOutput:
INTERSECT: Alice, Carol, Eve
EXCEPT(2025-2026): Bob, Dave
EXCEPT(2026-2025): Frank, Grace

description.task: Combine UNION, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT in a report that classifies customers across multiple time periods.
instructions: Run all INSERT and all three set operation queries.
Loyal customers (in all 3): Alice only (appears in Q1, Q2, Q3).
Q1-only churned: Carol ordered in Q1, not in Q2 or Q3... check the data.
Wait — Carol appears in Q2 too. Re-check: Dave is in Q1 only, so Dave = churned.
Total unique: Alice, Bob, Carol, Dave, Eve, Frank, Grace = 7.
Add a comment: what does 'customer churn rate' mean in a business context?
description.hint: INTERSECT of 3 sets: start with Q1∩Q2, then ∩Q3. Alice is the only name in all three. Total unique via UNION: 7 unique customers across the year.
description.expectedOutput:
Loyal: Alice
Churned after Q1: Dave (Dave in Q1, not Q2 or Q3)
Total unique: 7
